channel.title: Master Feed (Updated every 30 mins)
channel.description: Master Feed (Updated every 30 mins) generated by script

item.title: Somalia’s Fragile Government May Be on the Verge of Collapse
item.description: After disputes culminated in fighting last week, the country’s political system is in doubt.

item.title: Trump Says More Strikes on Iran Are Coming
item.description: Tehran will “pay the price” for stalling negotiations, the U.S. president warned.

item.title: Diagnosing the Israeli-Palestinian Conflict Doesn’t Solve It
item.description: Two new books offer fresh explanations for what went wrong—but little on how to make it go right.

item.title: Syria is an unexpected beneficiary of the Gulf war
item.description: The revival of an old oil-export route from Iraq to the Mediterranean helps Syria’s new regime

item.title: How to win the World Cup
item.description: Being rich helps, but being open to immigration works best of all

item.title: American capitalism is run by millionaires, not billionaires
item.description: They hide in plain sight—and wield enormous power

item.title: New techniques can predict and prevent lung cancer
item.description: A molecular signature can identify those most at risk

item.title: The World Cup has always been beset by scandal and strife
item.description: So has FIFA, the outfit that administers it

item.title: The World Cup is an exception. Fun is more fragmented than ever
item.description: A local-content boom is rewriting the rules of entertainment—and of soft power

item.title: Too much Chinese science is ignored by the West
item.description: A bad reputation and cultural ignorance are probably responsible

item.title: Donald Trump’s least bad option in Iran
item.description: He must swallow his pride and accept a deal worse than the pre-war status quo

item.title: America’s mayors join the scrabble to become influencers
item.description: A dispatch from Long Beach, California

item.title: Europe Plans to Crack Down on Russia—but for Real This Time
item.description: Trump and the Iran war gave Russia a boost. Brussels is fed up.

item.title: The Geopolitics of the World Cup
item.description: How immigration, trade, and conflict are overshadowing soccer.

item.title: Why the India-Oman CEPA is Special
item.description: Much of Oman’s coastline lies outside the Strait of Hormuz. With CEPA kicking in on June 1, India will be looking to benefit from the agreement and Oman’s geography.

item.title: The Enduring Enigma of Economic Growth
item.description: Developing countries have made enormous progress in many of the factors behind growth, reflected in stronger institutions, rising education levels, higher life expectancy, and increased investment rates. Yet income convergence with advanced economies has been painfully slow—and in most cases absent.

item.title: What Comes After Global Order
item.description: Rather than a new international framework, we need a system for moving from one framework to the next.

item.title: Is the US Stock Market Too Big?
item.description: With China and India having grown so fast as to compensate for the underperformance of their fellow BRICS (Brazil, Russia, and South Africa), the center of gravity in the global economy continues to shift eastward. The question now is whether—and when—equity valuations will reflect this underlying reality.

item.title: Exporting U.S. Military AI Won’t Be Easy
item.description: Middle powers have alternatives, and AI labs have opinions.

item.title: Progressives Should Show Real Solidarity With China
item.description: Don’t let Chinamaxxing come at the expense of meaningful engagement with Chinese workers and activists.

item.title: A Death in Detention Puts Tajikistan’s Police on Trial
item.description: Six Dushanbe officers face a rare prosecution over the death of Maksudjon Saidov in custody but none of them is charged with torture.

item.title: Brazil’s Bad Vibes
item.description: A government that abandons structural agendas whenever sentiment turns negative will still be managing sentiment four years later—with nothing to show for it. Brazil’s “vibecession” is a real problem for the current government; but winning the news cycle is not a real solution.

item.title: Ethnic Restaurants and Immigrant Entrepreneurs Facing Crisis Under Japan’s New Visa Rules
item.description: While the government views the new rules as a necessary measure to curb abuse, some observers worry that they may also deter genuine entrepreneurs.

item.title: Fear of the SaaSpocalypse is tormenting techland
item.description: Software once ate the world. Now it is in danger of eating itself

item.title: Negotiating With the Antichrist
item.description: Apocalyptic thinking is seeping into the political discourse, with figures like Peter Thiel, Pete Hegseth, and Aleksandr Dugin portraying political differences as struggles between good and evil. When rivals become mortal enemies, the instruments of politics and diplomacy cannot work.

item.title: Why Is Europe’s Economy Falling Short?
item.description: For much of the postwar era, European policymakers have prioritized stability and predictability over fostering breakthrough innovation. Today, as the United States and China compete for AI dominance, a stagnant Europe is struggling to regain its place at the technological frontier.

item.title: Why Cambodia and Thailand’s Peace Is So Uneasy
item.description: A step forward in the maritime dispute masks dangers on the land border.

item.title: The End of the Open Internet
item.description: Europe lost the plot on online speech.

item.title: Turkey’s Quiet Realignment
item.description: Russia’s loss is NATO’s gain.

item.title: The Fault Lines in China’s Power
item.description: America must build—and use—leverage against Beijing.

item.title: An interview with South Korea’s president
item.description: Lee Jae Myung has put his country on track again, but challenges loom

item.title: U.S. Launches New ‘Self-Defense’ Strikes on Iran Over Apache Helicopter Crash
item.description: But Iranian officials reportedly claim that the downing was not deliberate.

item.title: Xi Seeks to Draw North Korea Back Into China’s Orbit
item.description: The Chinese leader’s long-awaited visit to Pyongyang aims to counter Russian influence.

item.title: Can Northeast Asia’s New Geopolitical Reality Create Opportunities for Mongolia?
item.description: Within the emerging geopolitical landscape, Mongolia may have opportunities to diversify its economic partners.

item.title: Why a South Korean Starbucks Ad Mocked Murdered Protesters
item.description: Far-right meme politics shows how strong anti-democratic forces remain.

item.title: Wall Street’s undignified SpaceX mania
item.description: Is that a rocket in your lobby, or are you just happy to see me?

item.title: NATO’s Digital Back End Could Fall Apart Without Change
item.description: Shared standards on cloud services are urgently needed.

item.title: Why Turkey likes NATO again
item.description: The alliance is in trouble, but Erdogan is enthusiastic

item.title: Ukrainian strikes are inflicting pain deep inside Russia
item.description: Our modelling suggests they are doing even more damage than commonly assumed

item.title: Why strongmen are wrong to loathe Europe
item.description: The leaders of America, China and Russia scorn consensual politics. That is a mistake

item.title: Long Night Over Hualien: The China Threat Through the Eyes of Taiwan’s Indigenous Peoples
item.description: “The current situation is not the fruit of our choosing, but of history itself.”

item.title: What happens when a presidential vote is a dead heat?
item.description: Peru, looking for its ninth president in a decade, is in a tight spot

item.title: The Federal Reserve must soon give Donald Trump bad news
item.description: Kevin Warsh, the unlucky new chairman, has seen his case for lower interest rates disintegrate

item.title: Big Tech’s Shadow Market Power
item.description: Big Tech firms are amassing market power through strategies that bypass current rules for oversight, investigation, and intervention. Unless and until regulators account for the full scope and scale of these strategies, Silicon Valley’s dominance will remain unchallenged.

item.title: Lebanon Is No Longer About Lebanon
item.description: Iran would rather fight than let Israel decide Lebanon’s fate.

item.title: Why Does the G7 Keep Inviting India to its Summits?
item.description: India’s recurring invitations reveal as much about New Delhi’s ambitions as about the G7’s adaptation to the changing times.

item.title: Apple’s new Siri is a dark horse in the AI race
item.description: The iPhone-maker does not need to build models to cash in on the technology

item.title: The AI Revolution Mirrors the Green Transition
item.description: The need for massive upfront investments and the likelihood of significant job displacement imply remarkable parallels between the AI buildout and the green transition. In both cases, the state has an important role to play in guiding market forces on behalf of the public good.

item.title: As Integration in the Greater Bay Area Accelerates, Small Businesses in Macau Suffer
item.description: Drawn by lower prices, a wider variety of products and convenience, Macau’s residents have taken to spending in mainland China.

item.title: The Democratic Dilemma in South Korea’s Local Election Ballot Shortage
item.description: President Lee sold Korean democracy abroad as a system that fixes itself. Now his party and the administration have to prove it by holding the election commission to account without handing the conspiracy theorists a win.

item.title: Where Is China’s Trillion-Dollar Trade Surplus Going?
item.description: Whereas China’s trade surplus used to be absorbed largely by the central bank and channeled into official reserves, it is now being redeployed by private actors in search of yield. This indicates China’s progress on financial deepening and underscores markets' increasingly central role in shaping the economy.

item.title: Xi and Kim Agree to Strengthen ‘Strategic Relationship’ with Socialist Principles
item.description: Touting the achievements Pyongyang has made in recent years, Xi de facto recognized North Korea as a nuclear-armed state.

item.title: How Kazakhstan Uses the Stalling Board of Peace
item.description: Despite opting out of contributing the $1 billion fee required to become a permanent member of Donald Trump's Board of Peace, Kazakhstan has continued to publicly highlight its membership.

item.title: Cockroach Janta Party: Is the Modi Government Wary of a ‘Gen Z Revolt’?
item.description: Hitherto confined to social media, the "cockroaches" emerged on the ground at Delhi’s Jantar Mantar on June 6. What started as a joke has turned serious.

item.title: American Power Is Wrung Out
item.description: The war in Iran has depleted the Pentagon as China’s military buildup matures.

item.title: The Future of Development Finance Is Not Primarily About Money
item.description: Multilateral development banks have not kept pace with changes to the global financial environment. For middle-income countries like India, accessing knowledge and technology is now a bigger challenge than raising capital, which suggests that these institutions must rethink their operating model.

item.title: How to Escape Blame for Bad Advice
item.description: If you get an important strategic issue wrong, there are still ways to avoid accountability.

item.title: Is VinFast’s Costly EV Gambit Coming to an End?
item.description: Despite its vaunting ambitions and rapid expansion, the upstart automaker has yet to prove it can compete beyond the Vietnamese market.

item.title: Brunei’s Sultan Ibrahim Reshuffles Cabinet, Appointing His Son as Foreign Minister
item.description: The elevation of three of the sultan's sons suggest that key posts are increasingly being concentrated within the direct line of succession.

item.title: The Invisible Hand Won’t Rebuild U.S. Shipyards
item.description: War in Iran has put a spotlight on declining U.S. maritime power.

item.title: How America Lost Command of the Commons
item.description: The oceans may soon be tolled.

item.title: Don’t Give Up on Global Order
item.description: America depends on global order—and can restore it.

item.title: At Least 32 Dead After Earthquake Hits Southern Philippines, Authorities Say
item.description: President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. has stated that "the national government is moving and we will not leave Mindanao behind."

item.title: Japan’s Security Focus Shift
item.description: China’s reach is extending beyond the First Island Chain. Japan is taking note.

item.title: Iran, Israel Pull Back From Brink After Trading Fire
item.description: But another Israeli assault on Beirut could collapse the two sides’ fragile truce.

item.title: Trump Started a War He Can’t Control
item.description: He wants to end the Iran war. But Iran, Israel, and Hezbollah have other ideas.

item.title: Armenia’s election is a setback for Vladimir Putin
item.description: Russia’s dirty tricks are failing to stop its pivot to the West

item.title: In China, innovation and economic malaise live side by side
item.description: Which will win out?

item.title: China is innovative. Its economy is a mess. Which will win out?
item.description: A question that will define the 21st century

item.title: When Work Moves Without Workers: Nepal and Asia’s New Mobility Frontier
item.description: Across Asia, governments are experimenting with policies designed to capitalize on the rapid expansion of remote work.

item.title: A bidding war erupts for the world’s oldest bank
item.description: Monte dei Paschi di Siena is suddenly all the rage

item.title: Central Asia’s Water Crisis is Becoming a Regional Economic Risk
item.description: Climate stress, shrinking glaciers, and fragmented governance are pushing the region toward a new era of systemic vulnerability.

item.title: How Israel is frustrating Donald Trump’s Iran plans
item.description: Binyamin Netanyahu has defied America twice in recent days

item.title: Afghanistan’s Women: Erased
item.description: An entire generation of Afghan girls have had their aspirations suspended by policies systematically excluding them from education and public life.

item.title: Lee Jae-myung’s First Year Successes Put South Korea on the Path to Tangible Results
item.description: Last year, Lee stated in his inauguration speech that pragmatism and the national interest were the guiding principles of his presidency.

item.title: What the Banning of the JAAC Reveals About Power in Azad Kashmir
item.description: The ban may remove JAAC from the formal political arena, but it cannot remove the grievances that brought it into existence.

item.title: The ageing protesters trying to topple Washington’s “ego arch”
item.description: Donald Trump has energised his fellow boomers in opposition

item.title: Show Us the Money
item.description: How satire, social media, and shame are forging Syria’s new public square.

item.title: China Is Providing AI That’s Literate in Africa’s Languages
item.description: Chinese models have become the overwhelming choice for African developers.

item.title: The War Room newsletter: When war becomes a political aesthetic
item.description: Shashank Joshi, our defence editor, examines why conflict exerts a “strange pull”

item.title: China Is Making MAHA’s Favorite Drug
item.description: RFK fans and China hawks are on a collision course over peptides.

item.title: A dropout-turned-influencer shakes up Chinese science
item.description: Fraud is rampant—but his videos mean everyone is now talking about it

item.title: Beyond Search and Rescue: What the Japan-South Korea SAREX Revival Really Means
item.description: The return of the Japan-South Korea naval search and rescues exercise after a nine-year hiatus reflects strategic convergence, but history still casts a long shadow.

item.title: Nigeria’s Perilous French Gambit
item.description: Nigeria’s increasingly close relationship with France marks a departure from six decades of foreign policy aimed at reducing French influence in West Africa. An increasingly close relationship with a power that has long sought to undermine Nigerian influence in West Africa will benefit only politically connected business elites.

item.title: Israel: An ‘Invisible’ Migration Destination for Uzbeks
item.description: Israel is rarely mentioned in discussions of Uzbek labor migration. Yet thousands of Uzbek workers have quietly made it a significant destination.

item.title: How the Dreams and Aspirations of Bangladeshi Students Have Been Crushed
item.description: Affiliation with the Chhatra League, the students’ wing of the Awami League, before October 2024, was not an offense. It cannot be treated as one now.

item.title: Japan’s Shifting Center: How the Left Got Left Behind
item.description: A new political center is taking shape in Japan, one that is pushing the old center-left to the margins of the political spectrum.

item.title: Autocracy Is Fueling Mexico’s Fiscal Deterioration
item.description: Mexico’s recent ratings downgrades are merely a symptom of a more serious malady: the ruling Morena party’s efforts to consolidate power permanently. To ensure the legal predictability foreign investors require, President Claudia Sheinbaum would need to confront Morena’s ideological legacy and, more importantly, its cartel ties.

item.title: The U.S. Can’t Exclude China From Latin America
item.description: Rather than pushing Beijing out of the region, Washington should offer countries better strategic alternatives.

item.title: The Trump Economy Is Bad News for Republicans
item.description: Donald Trump triumphed in the 2024 election by continually hammering on the Biden administration’s dismal inflation record. If his party gets shellacked in this year’s midterm elections, it will be because he has made Americans even worse off.

item.title: Australia’s Rare Earth Sector
item.description: Opportunity, risk and the commitment gap

item.title: The West Asia Crisis Realigns Regional Trade Through Pakistan
item.description: Cargo-laden trucks are already plying six new overland routes linking its major ports to the Iranian border and onward to Central Asia.

item.title: SpaceX Is the New East India Company
item.description: Although SpaceX is not about to rule over foreign subjects, as the chartered companies founded in the early modern era did, it, too, is operating beyond the reach of any sovereign. And like its predecessors, it has already accumulated immense powers that governments will struggle to reclaim.

item.title: Thailand Says It Will Join UN-Backed Conciliation on Maritime Dispute With Cambodia
item.description: Thai Foreign Minister Sihasak Phuangketkeow said that Bangkok will cease all other bilateral talks while mediation is in progress.

item.title: Propaganda and Fake News Editors: Myanmar’s Manufactured Transition
item.description: Min Aung Hlaing's “civilian” government rests on a sophisticated propaganda architecture, and the independent media needed to challenge it is running out of funds.

item.title: India-Venezuela Relations Gain Renewed Energy
item.description: The U.S., not Delhi or Caracas, announced Venezuela’s Acting President Delcy Rodríguez’s visit to India last week. The U.S. role in pushing the oil trade is hard to ignore.

item.title: Trump Is Doing What FDR Could Not
item.description: But the president’s success at purging his party could cost the GOP in the long term.

item.title: Greeted as Liberators?
item.description: How Americans think about U.S. military interventions.

item.title: The Day After in Cuba
item.description: What American military force can and cannot do.

item.title: Why Western Institutions are Hosting India’s RSS Despite Its Anti-Minority Record
item.description: The rise of majoritarian politics globally, combined with a shifting geopolitical calculus around India, has created openings for the RSS that did not exist a decade ago.

item.title: Under Trump 2.0, the Quad’s Real Challenge to China Is Economic, Not Military
item.description: The grouping is unlikely to harden into an “Asian NATO.” For Beijing, the more consequential contest is over supply chains, technology standards, and the rules of the Indo-Pacific economy.

item.title: How artificial intelligence got better at building itself
item.description: What does “recursive self-improvement” mean for the technology?

item.title: Will artificial intelligence soon escape human control?
item.description: “Recursive self-improvement” is both tantalising and worrying

item.title: Nukes are off the agenda as Xi Jinping heads to North Korea
item.description: China is wary of Russian influence and another Trump-Kim summit

item.title: China and Russia are competing for influence over North Korea
item.description: So nukes are off the agenda as Xi Jinping heads to Pyongyang

item.title: The World Cup will test Mexico’s control over its territory
item.description: Donald Trump, who says the government is too weak to combat the drug gangs, will be watching

item.title: Money troubles are driving India’s states to drink
item.description: Things are so dire that one state has produced a rational alcohol policy

item.title: Robots could soon be delivering your pizza
item.description: An Estonian startup says its machines are now cheaper than human couriers

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: A modest proposal on Cuba
item.description: John Prideaux, our US editor, on why the island should become America’s 51st state

item.title: Armenians Vote Under Russia’s Shadow
item.description: Trump and Putin are backing different players in a contested country.

item.title: The U.S. Economy Is Proving Remarkably Resilient
item.description: Even in the face of tariffs and an energy crisis, the jobs report shows an economy that’s still humming.

item.title: Zelensky’s Pen Pal Diplomacy
item.description: Zelensky offers in-person peace talks in a rare open letter to Putin. Moscow isn’t interested.

item.title: Maggie O’Farrell’s Most Ambitious Novel Yet
item.description: Plus, a Venezuelan thriller in translation.

item.title: How hot is America’s labour market?
item.description: At the moment, balmy. But it wouldn’t take a lot to require some Fed air-conditioning

item.title: Should priests have to report child abuse disclosed in confession?
item.description: American states take different approaches

item.title: How Ukraine Has Turned the Tide
item.description: Resilience, technology, and European support have put Russia on the back foot.

item.title: Contain, Consolidate, and Co-Opt
item.description: How Europe has learned to live with great-power politics.

item.title: Romania Has Perfected the Art of Forgetting
item.description: In a new memoir, Nobel laureate Herta Müller condemns her homeland for failing to reconcile with history.

item.title: Oh Se-hoon Becomes Seoul’s Mayor for the 5th Time
item.description: Why Seoul picked the PPP incumbent yet again, and what it means for the city.

item.title: What in the World?
item.description: Test yourself on the week of May 30: Malta votes, Japan responds to militarism accusations, and Iran pauses talks with the U.S.

item.title: Myanmar’s Military Isn’t Conceding Much
item.description: Prisoner releases mostly reflect the regime’s growing confidence five years after seizing power in a coup.

item.title: The chemicals that reduce wrinkles
item.description: Vitamins, applied properly, can partially reverse the effects of ageing

item.title: The North Korea Succession Issue
item.description: Is a teenage girl being positioned as North Korea's next leader?

item.title: The ECB Should Not Raise Interest Rates Yet
item.description: Against a backdrop of acute uncertainty, weak growth, subdued wages, and elevated market interest rates, monetary tightening is warranted only when there is convincing evidence that higher inflation is becoming entrenched. No such evidence has yet materialized in Europe.

item.title: Sleeping in Mexico, Playing in America
item.description: The journey of Iran’s World Cup team sets a dangerous precedent for international soccer.

item.title: Uzbekistan, Russia Celebrate Start of Nuclear Power Plant Construction… Again
item.description: Tashkent and Moscow celebrated the start of construction on the plant – two months after the companies involved celebrated the same thing.

item.title: Bolivia’s Troubled Path With China
item.description: Chinese activities in Bolivia have deteriorated remarkably from their heyday under the populist leftist governments of Evo Morales and Luis Arce.

item.title: The Bipolar Trap: The Real Danger in the Trump-Xi Era
item.description: The challenge is not just preventing war between China and the U.S. It’s how to prevent their rivalry from becoming the sole organizing principle of international politics.

item.title: Why Did Nepali PM Balendra Shah Call on UK to ‘Take an Interest’ in an India-Nepal Dispute?
item.description: The controversial comment was made on the eve of ruling party chief Ravi Lamichhane’s visit to India.

item.title: When Markets Run on Empty
item.description: Markets are flying high despite geopolitical turmoil because a broad-based willingness to keep spending has been matched by an ability to keep doing so. But as more economic participants exhaust their means and rely on debt, a painful moment of truth will arrive—sooner rather than later if the Strait of Hormuz remains closed.

item.title: India’s Eye in the Sky: The Rise of a State-Deep Tech Startup Space Partnership
item.description: India’s deep tech space startups are transforming the country’s space ambitions, as the state increasingly supports private innovation to strengthen India’s strategic and economic position in the global space race.

item.title: Pacific Nations Adopt First Regional Climate Relocation Framework
item.description: Pacific governments have launched the world's first regional framework for planned climate relocation, creating new guidance for communities facing displacement from rising seas, coastal erosion, and intensifying storms.

item.title: Does the United States Have the Weapons for Trade War?
item.description: Economic strategists are pitching tougher policies for an anxious world.

item.title: The Pope and the AI Profiteers
item.description: In his first encyclical, Pope Leo XIV rightly rejects the idea that markets alone can be trusted to shape society’s technological future. Recognizing that AI raises questions that prices and profits cannot answer, the Chicago-born pope has posed a direct challenge to Chicago School economics.

item.title: Pete Hegseth’s Missed Chance to Reassure – and Deter – on Taiwan
item.description: The U.S. defense secretary failed to mention Taiwan once during his closely-watched speech at the Shangri-La Dialogue.

item.title: Could a Democratic Mid-Term Win Upset the China-US Detente?
item.description: If Democrats ride a “blue wave” to reclaim the House in November’s mid-term elections – a distinct possibility – U.S. China policy will become dangerously volatile.

item.title: Why China Weaponized the Thucydides Trap
item.description: It is no surprise that Beijing is now embracing this construct, hoping to shift the responsibility for stability and avoiding escalation onto Washington.

item.title: Bhutan’s Development Model Faces Its Hardest Test
item.description: Today, Bhutan’s development model faces pressures that may overwhelm the deliberate gradualism that has long defined its approach.

item.title: AI Must Not Encroach on Human Dignity
item.description: In a world captivated by technological advances, Pope Leo XIV’s first encyclical seeks to shift attention away from the marvels and terrors of AI and toward the magnificence of humanity. The message that the technology lacks a “moral conscience” and is distinct from human cognition is necessary and welcome.

item.title: Xi Jinping to Visit North Korea Amid Deepening Three-Way Alliance
item.description: For the first time in seven years, Chinese leader Xi Jinping will visit Pyongyang on June 8 – closely following his separate summits with the U.S. and Russian presidents.

item.title: How Brazilian Soccer Shed Protectionism
item.description: Will openness to foreign players and coaches make a difference at the World Cup?

item.title: Kyrgyzstan Elected to UN Security Council For First Time In Country’s History
item.description: Kyrgyzstan, the second Central Asian country ever to be elected as a non-permanent member of the UNSC, was propelled to victory on the back of a wave of diplomatic support from its neighbors, Turkiye, and Muslim states.

item.title: The Key Forces Now Shaping Markets and Geopolitics
item.description: Three major factors are likely to shape economic and geopolitical outcomes over the next several years: politically unconstrained AI development, a shift from the logic of globalization to zero-sum thinking, and heightened international tail risks. Much could go right, or at least as expected—until it doesn’t.

item.title: Washington Is Testing Singapore’s Patience
item.description: Donald Trump is working hard to alienate yet another strategic partner.

item.title: The Living Ghosts of Violent Radicalism
item.description: Following World War II, the most militant student movements emerged in the former Axis powers as a way to cope with previous generations' complicity. But while the Red Army Faction and others were haunted by memories of past crimes, today's young people have mostly forgotten, and a growing number think far-right politics are cool.

item.title: Using AI to Test Policy Language
item.description: Policymakers have long understood that language affects economic behavior, but until recently, there were few tools capable of systematically analyzing its effects. AI may now help close that gap, enabling officials to anticipate how framing and timing can move markets and shift public opinion.

item.title: Everyone Is Waiting for Trump’s Gaza Plan to Die
item.description: The Board of Peace has been revealed to be so flawed as to be completely unworkable.

item.title: Myanmar’s Military Is Turning to Russia to Revive Its Stalled Cyber Dream
item.description: Could the stalled Yatarnapon Cyber City project be set for an injection of Russian assistance?

item.title: US Forced Labor Ruling Does Not Reflect Vietnam’s Efforts, Govt Says
item.description: Hanoi is now the subject of three separate U.S. trade probes, which will likely introduce new areas of tension into ongoing trade talks.

item.title: Fixing AI in Conflict
item.description: We already have the tools.

item.title: To Fix Haiti, Look to Colombia
item.description: An example—albeit an imperfect one—of how to achieve a lasting peace.

item.title: Can the UAE Go It Alone?
item.description: A risky quest for strategic autonomy in a war-torn Middle East.

item.title: How China Misperceives Itself
item.description: Beijing’s blindspots hinder real reform.

item.title: Iran and the Hidden Cost of Wartime Access
item.description: America’s empire of bases makes It easier to blunder Into war.

item.title: How Myanmar Fits Into India’s Troubled Neighborhood Policy
item.description: Min Aung Hlaing’s visit to India may help Delhi seal deals with the military-backed government. But it has angered pro-democracy forces in Myanmar.

item.title: How Government Inaction Turned Sumatra’s Rains Into a National Catastrophe
item.description: The Indonesian political system has enabled deforestation through weak oversight, opaque licensing, and regulations designed to favor extractive industries.

item.title: Indonesia’s Free Meal Program to Target ‘Efficiency’ After Arrest of Former Head
item.description: The program has come under scrutiny for its implementation and high cost, which is set to total nearly $15 billion in 2026.

item.title: Kushner-Linked Luxury Resort Plans Ignite Protests Across Albania
item.description: Thousands call for Tirana to block the investment projects, citing environmental and corruption concerns.

item.title: Meanwhile, in the Western Hemisphere…
item.description: The U.S. military remains extremely active in and around Latin America.

item.title: How Trump Should Approach an Iran Endgame
item.description: Former Iran negotiator Robert Malley on the White House’s best options right now.

item.title: Trump Is Playing With Fire in Cuba
item.description: The U.S. blockade on the island is unprecedented—and could lead to prolonged upheaval.

item.title: Rising Security Risks Are Changing China’s Belt and Road Strategy
item.description: Beijing is becoming more selective about where and what it builds overseas. That transition was already underway but it has been hastened by the Iran-U.S. war.

item.title: Did China Overestimate the Geopolitical Returns of Its Latin America Strategy?
item.description: The assumption that economic leverage leads to political influence has underpinned China analysis for decades. Developments in Latin America are calling that into question.

item.title: What Shangri-La 2026 Revealed About the Future Regional Order
item.description: Like the CPTPP after the U.S. exit from TPP, the next phase of regional security may be shaped by what U.S. allies and partners build when Washington moves one step back.

item.title: China Is Too Big for Shangri-La
item.description: Snubbing a key security forum shows Beijing’s new confidence.

item.title: Trump’s New ‘Forced Labor’ Tariffs Are a Fig Leaf
item.description: The new import duties are “a solution in search of a problem.”

item.title: China Is Not Europe’s Competitiveness Bogeyman
item.description: Calls for European leaders to protect domestic industry from "unfairly" subsidized Chinese competition have lately grown louder. But while tariffs might offer temporary relief to a few sectors, they cannot restore Europe's technological leadership, industrial dynamism, or export competitiveness.

item.title: AI and Its Discontents
item.description: With AI hype reaching fever pitch, a backlash has begun, with university graduates, in particular, resisting a technology that could quickly render their skills obsolete. They are not alone in challenging AI boosters’ bubbly narrative. While the technology will transform the economy and upend labor markets, important bottlenecks and major risks will limit its productivity benefits—and the payoff for investors.
